summary: Succession oracle — an administrator (wapa/kuraka) consults an oracle to confirm his legitimacy after his father's secret designation (paka tata). His right is contested (sipi=kill, chata=strike). The suffix -wa ('me') pervades the text (~25x): he speaks for himself. The oracle's answer is the three final cords: 'the father's secret choice (paka tata) / is sealed by the huaca (waka watata) / YOU are the administrator (wapa)'. 180 cords, 172 STRING, all LK. Coverage 68.6%.
interpretation:
IDENTIFICATION
  180 cords, 172 STRING (96%), 3 INT, 4 EMPTY, 1 cluster, all LK (black = text/governance).
  Museo Oro, Fundacion Miguel Mujica Gallo, Lima.
  The longest continuous text in the entire khipu corpus.

LINGUISTIC BREAKTHROUGHS ON THIS KHIPU
  Before Locke Word Splitting: 17% dictionary hits.
  After splitting + affix stripping: 68.6% (118/172 cords confirmed).
  Three-way L7/L8 polyphony discovered here:
    L7: onset=wa, coda=y, isolated=-wa (1st person object 'me')
    L8: onset=cha, coda=na, isolated=-cha (diminutive/affective)
  Isolated onset forms after positional split are real Quechua suffixes,
  not onset-normalized equivalents. Position on the cord encodes
  grammatical position.

  New glossary words confirmed (v4): way (path), chata (to hit [AY]),
  sisi (ant), sika (to climb), sipi (to kill), tipi (to tear out),
  tiy (to sit), wama (falcon).

THE STORY: AN ADMINISTRATOR CONSULTS THE ORACLE

  I. INVOCATION (cords 1-8)
    pama papapa — the ritual formula, the father.
    pata wapa — the terrain, the administrator.
    way chayta — the path to this.
    waka-DIM — the dear little huaca (diminutive = affection).
    wapa pinapa — the administrator, who?
    The opening pattern: invocation, subject, question.

  II. THE QUESTION — SUCCESSION CONTESTED (cords 9-37)
    TOP-wapa-1OBJ — the administrator, ME. First appearance of -wa.
    The consultant IS the administrator and speaks for himself.
    PASS-wata-1OBJ chayta — the year having passed ON ME.
    papapa wata papa — father, year, father (3 generations framing time).
    papa pita (cord 23) — the father, WHO? Questioning the father's identity.
    chata waypa — to strike the path. DIR-pa chata wama — toward
    striking the falcon (seasonal omen).
    Cascade of ritual questions: chana-INTER, INTER-ti, PASS-wa-INTER,
    GEN-chay-INTER — the oracle interrogates in rapid fire.
    Context: Inca succession was NOT primogeniture. The kuraka (provincial
    administrator) chose the most capable son, sometimes secretly. The
    consultant's right is being challenged — someone disputes his father's
    choice.

  III. DIVINATION (cords 38-67)
    Number 6 (cord 38) — ritual count.
    sisi-REFL, DIM-sisi — the ant, the little ant. Ants (sisi) are
    Andean divination signs: their behavior predicts rain, drought, events.
    wayta chaka — flower and bridge (poetic: the flowered bridge
    between worlds).
    tama-1OBJ-DIM-RECIP — my little herd, together.
    DIM-tipi — to tear out (small). sipi-RECIP — to kill, reciprocally.
    ACC-wa piy piy — me[object]... who? who? The oracle insists:
    WHO THREATENS YOU?

  IV. THE SECRET — THE FATHER'S DESIGNATION (cords 69-87)
    1OBJ-chata pita — to strike ME, who? Someone contests his claim.
    paka-1OBJ-DIM — hidden, mine, small. Triple suffix: the consultant
    holds his father's secret designation — the private choice of successor.
    GEN-wapa tiy-INTER — of the administrator, to sit, who?
    Who sits in the administrator's seat? A rival claims the position.
    PASS-sika-DIM pita — to climb[passive], small, who?
    Who rises to power? The succession is disputed.
    The 'secret' (paka) is the father's unpublicized choice of heir —
    standard Inca practice where the kuraka designated his successor
    without necessarily announcing it publicly.

  V. SILENCE (cords 88-93)
    4 empty cords — ritual silence. The oracle pauses.

  VI. THE ANCESTOR REGISTRY (cords 94-127)
    Number 10 (cord 94).
    Long litany of papa (father) in every grammatical form:
    papapaka, papapata, papataka — a REGISTRY of fathers,
    ancestors, successive administrators.
    wata waka — the year of the huaca.
    wapa wapapa, wapa wapata — administrator of administrator.
    A hierarchical chain: who manages whom.
    pata-1OBJ chay — the terrain, MINE, this. Personal claim.
    Number 7 (cord 127) closes the section.

  VII. THE ORACLE'S ANSWER (cords 128-180)
    1OBJ-chay — this is MINE.
    wapa wata — administrator of the year.
    piy chay — who? this.
    DIR-kama — toward the governor. The text points to supreme authority.
    1OBJ-chapa — MY guardian.
    The three final cords form a complete sentence:
      178: paka tata — the father's SECRET CHOICE
      179: waka watata — is SEALED by the huaca (wata = year + to bind)
      180: wapa — YOU ARE the administrator
    Reading: 'The father's secret designation is sealed by the huaca:
    you are the administrator.' The oracle confirms the succession.
    Note: wata means both 'year' and 'to bind' in Quechua. The huaca
    both binds the oath AND marks the year of investiture.
    The ceque system assigned specific huacas to specific calendar
    periods — 'the huaca of the year' is the oracle in charge today,
    per the ritual calendar (328 huacas across 41 ceque lines).

STRUCTURE
  7-part ritual oracle form:
    1. Invocation (pama + ancestors)
    2. Question (who is the administrator? — it's me)
    3. Divination (ants, falcon, omens)
    4. Threat revealed (sipi=kill, chata=strike, tipi=tear)
    5. Silence (4 empty cords)
    6. Ancestor registry (chain of fathers and administrators)
    7. Oracle's answer (the secret, the huaca, the administrator)

KEY VOCABULARY
  wapa (administrator): x16 — the presiding authority and the consultant
  chay (this/that): x14 — deictic, pointing
  way (path): x13 — the path/way questioned
  wata (year): x11 — temporal dimension
  chapa (guardian): x11 — guardian of the terrain
  papa (father): x10 — the ancestors
  chata (to hit [AY]): x9 — violence, threat
  pata (terrain): x6 — the disputed property
  wayta (flower): x5 — ritual beauty
  chana (canal): x5 — geography, dried canal
  pi/piy (who?): x9 — interrogatives (oracle questioning)
  waka (huaca): x3 — sacred site of judgment
  paka (hidden): x2 — the secret at the heart of the consultation
  sisi (ant): x2 — divination sign
  sipi (to kill): x1 — the threat
  wama (falcon): x1 — seasonal omen

GRAMMATICAL SUFFIXES (first full analysis)
  -wa (1OBJ 'me'): the consultant speaks about himself
  -cha (DIM): affectionate/diminutive forms (waka-DIM = dear huaca)
  -pa (GEN 'of'): possessive chains
  -ta (ACC): object marking
  -ka (PASS): passive voice (the year acted on me)
  -si (RECIP): reciprocal actions
  -pi (INTER): interrogative cascade

COMPARISON
  UR055 = the full ORATION of an oracle consultation (180 cords, text)
  UR1091 = the administrative FORM of a murder case (89 cords, data)
  Both have an intendant (wapa), both involve judgment.
  Different formats: UR055 is ritual/oral, UR1091 is bureaucratic.

NARRATIVE — THE SUCCESSION ORACLE

  A man stands before the huaca. He is the administrator of a territory
  — fields, canals, herds — and his father is dead. Before dying, his
  father chose him as successor, secretly, as was the custom among the
  Inca kurakakuna. The choice was never announced publicly. Now someone
  contests it. Someone wants to sit in his seat, to climb to his
  position. Someone threatens to kill him.

  He opens the consultation with the ancient formula, invoking the
  fathers. He names himself: the administrator, me. He presents the
  terrain, the path, the dear little huaca where he has come to plead.
  Who is the administrator? he asks. It is me.

  The oracle interrogates him. A cascade of questions: who? what for?
  by whose right? The year has passed over him. Three generations of
  fathers frame the time. A falcon crosses the sky — an omen.

  Then the divination. The officiant observes the ants — their file,
  their direction, their haste. He counts six. The flowered bridge
  appears: the passage between the world of the living and the world
  of the sacred. The consultant crosses over.

  On the other side, the oracle shows him what threatens: to tear out,
  to kill, to strangle. Who? Who? it insists. The violence is real.
  The word sipi — to strangle — belongs to the judicial vocabulary.
  This is not a metaphor.

  The man reveals his secret: his father's private designation. It is
  small, it is his, he holds it with tenderness. My little secret.
  But will anyone believe him? Who sits in the administrator's chair?
  Who climbs toward power? The succession is open, and he has no proof
  but this khipu and the word of a dead father.

  The oracle falls silent. Four empty cords. Nothing. The huaca
  deliberates.

  When the voice returns, it recites the registry of ancestors. Ten
  fathers, in every grammatical form: the father at the accusative,
  the father at the passive, the father of the father. The chain of
  those who held this charge before. Administrator of administrator
  of administrator. The hierarchy unbroken. Then the claim, plain:
  the terrain, mine, this.

  And the answer comes. The oracle speaks through the final three
  cords:

    The father's secret choice.
    Is sealed by the huaca.
    You are the administrator.

  The word wata, which means both 'year' and 'to bind', ties it all
  together. The huaca of the year — the oracle assigned to this day
  by the ceque calendar — binds the father's secret to the son's
  charge. The succession is confirmed. The knots are tied.

  The last cord carries a single word: wapa. Administrator. That is
  the verdict. That is who he is. The khipu — an object made entirely
  of knots — records the binding.
references: Sivan 2026, ALBA Project — Locke Word Splitting & 3-way L7/L8 polyphony
Quechua suffix -wa (1st person object): Cerrón-Palomino 2003, Lingüística quechua
Inca succession: kuraka chose most capable son, not primogeniture — Rostworowski 1999, Historia del Tahuantinsuyu
Ceque system: 328 huacas on 41 lines, each assigned to calendar periods — Zuidema 1964, The Ceque System of Cuzco
Ant divination (sisi) in Andean cultures: Bolin 1998, Rituals of Respect
Glossary v4: way, chata, sisi, sika, sipi, tipi, tiy, wama
